explanation_md: The using declaration `using std::cout;` is scoped to `printA()`, so `cout` works inside it. Back in `main()`, the fully-qualified `std::cout` is used, which also works. Both lines print normally, producing `A` then `B`.

The `using namespace std;` directive is scoped to the inner block. Inside that block `cout` is accessible. Outside the block the directive no longer applies, but `std::cout` still works because it is fully qualified. Both lines print successfully.

options.a: Both are equally fine — using directives always stay within one translation unit.
options.b: Option B is bad because every file that includes the header silently inherits all of std, risking name collisions.
options.c: Option A is bad because it puts the compiler into a slow name-lookup mode.
options.d: Neither is bad as long as the code compiles without errors.

explanation_md: A `using namespace` in a header pollutes every translation unit that includes it, making all names from `std` available without qualification everywhere — a major source of hard-to-diagnose ambiguity errors. In a self-contained `.cpp` file the blast radius is limited to that file, which is still not ideal but is far less harmful.

options.a: You cannot use `std::cout` and a local variable in the same expression.
options.b: The using declaration `using std::cout;` clashes with the local variable `cout` already declared in the same scope.
options.c: `int cout` is a reserved identifier that cannot be used as a variable name.
options.d: The `#include <iostream>` must appear after all `using` declarations.

explanation_md: A using declaration introduces a name into the current scope. Since `cout` is already declared as an `int` in the same scope, the compiler sees a redefinition and rejects it. The fix is to rename the local variable to something other than `cout`.

prompt_md: A using declaration such as `using std::cout;` placed inside `main()` — which of the following best describes its effect?

options.a: It makes `cout` available in all functions in the same file.
options.b: It makes `cout` available everywhere in the program after that point.
options.c: It makes `cout` available only within the block where the declaration appears.
options.d: It replaces `std::cout` with a new object named `cout`.

explanation_md: A using declaration respects normal C++ scope rules: it introduces the name into the current block scope and that name ceases to be visible once that block ends. It does not affect other functions or other scopes; `std::cout` and the introduced `cout` refer to the same underlying object — no new object is created.
